verses.text: 'O give thanks unto the LORD; for he is good,' and let Israel, the priests, and all who fear Him say, 'his mercy endureth for ever.' The refrain rings through the psalm. The enduring mercy of God is cause for endless thanks.

'O give thanks unto the LORD; for he is good,' and let Israel, the priests, and all who fear Him say, 'his mercy endureth for ever.' The refrain rings through the psalm. The enduring mercy of God is cause for endless thanks.

'O give thanks unto the LORD; for he is good,' and let Israel, the priests, and all who fear Him say, 'his mercy endureth for ever.' The refrain rings through the psalm. The enduring mercy of God is cause for endless thanks.

'O give thanks unto the LORD; for he is good,' and let Israel, the priests, and all who fear Him say, 'his mercy endureth for ever.' The refrain rings through the psalm. The enduring mercy of God is cause for endless thanks.

In distress he called and the LORD answered, setting him at large; 'the LORD is on my side; I will not fear: what can man do unto me?' God's nearness banishes fear. With the Lord as our helper, human threats lose their terror.

In distress he called and the LORD answered, setting him at large; 'the LORD is on my side; I will not fear: what can man do unto me?' God's nearness banishes fear. With the Lord as our helper, human threats lose their terror.

In distress he called and the LORD answered, setting him at large; 'the LORD is on my side; I will not fear: what can man do unto me?' God's nearness banishes fear. With the Lord as our helper, human threats lose their terror.
'It is better to trust in the LORD than to put confidence in man,' even in princes. God is the surer refuge. Human help may fail, but trust in God never disappoints.
'It is better to trust in the LORD than to put confidence in man,' even in princes. God is the surer refuge. Human help may fail, but trust in God never disappoints.
Surrounded by hostile nations like swarming bees, 'in the name of the LORD will I destroy them.' He conquers by God's name. The believer hemmed in by foes prevails through the strength of God.
Surrounded by hostile nations like swarming bees, 'in the name of the LORD will I destroy them.' He conquers by God's name. The believer hemmed in by foes prevails through the strength of God.
Surrounded by hostile nations like swarming bees, 'in the name of the LORD will I destroy them.' He conquers by God's name. The believer hemmed in by foes prevails through the strength of God.

Though hard pressed, the LORD helped him, becoming 'my strength and song, and... my salvation'; the right hand of the LORD does valiantly. Victory turns to song. The God who saves is Himself the theme of the rescued one's praise.

'I shall not die, but live, and declare the works of the LORD,' for though chastened sorely, he was not given over to death. Discipline did not destroy him. God's correcting hand spares His servant for further service.

'I shall not die, but live, and declare the works of the LORD,' for though chastened sorely, he was not given over to death. Discipline did not destroy him. God's correcting hand spares His servant for further service.

He asks for 'the gates of righteousness' to be opened, the gate of the LORD by which the righteous enter, giving thanks for his answered prayer. He longs to enter and worship. The way into God's presence is opened to the thankful righteous.

He asks for 'the gates of righteousness' to be opened, the gate of the LORD by which the righteous enter, giving thanks for his answered prayer. He longs to enter and worship. The way into God's presence is opened to the thankful righteous.

He asks for 'the gates of righteousness' to be opened, the gate of the LORD by which the righteous enter, giving thanks for his answered prayer. He longs to enter and worship. The way into God's presence is opened to the thankful righteous.

'The stone which the builders refused is become the head stone of the corner'; this is the LORD's doing, and 'this is the day which the LORD hath made.' Christ is that rejected, exalted Stone. The day of His triumph calls forth rejoicing.

'The stone which the builders refused is become the head stone of the corner'; this is the LORD's doing, and 'this is the day which the LORD hath made.' Christ is that rejected, exalted Stone. The day of His triumph calls forth rejoicing.

'The stone which the builders refused is become the head stone of the corner'; this is the LORD's doing, and 'this is the day which the LORD hath made.' Christ is that rejected, exalted Stone. The day of His triumph calls forth rejoicing.

'Save now' — Hosanna — and 'Blessed be he that cometh in the name of the LORD,' the crowd's cry at Christ's entry. They welcome the coming King. The Saviour men once rejected is hailed as the blessed One who comes from God.

'Save now' — Hosanna — and 'Blessed be he that cometh in the name of the LORD,' the crowd's cry at Christ's entry. They welcome the coming King. The Saviour men once rejected is hailed as the blessed One who comes from God.

'Save now' — Hosanna — and 'Blessed be he that cometh in the name of the LORD,' the crowd's cry at Christ's entry. They welcome the coming King. The Saviour men once rejected is hailed as the blessed One who comes from God.

'Thou art my God, and I will praise thee,' he ends, returning to the opening refrain: 'O give thanks unto the LORD; for he is good.' Praise frames the psalm. All deliverance flows back to thanksgiving for God's enduring mercy.
